speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Berlin, and I felt that I have to find a way to get out. I had relatives in Cuba, and I kept their name very close to my heart. I felt that eventually, my future is going to be not back to Lithuania. And I-- I started thinking about how to formulate and how to escape. Next to my-- next to our compound was a-- a building, a commercial building, and there was a Jewish person whose name was Helmut Selton, and he owned a-- he was a fur-- he made furs. And his wife was German, and I became very friendly with them. I, on many occasions, I mean-- I helped him to get a car. I helped him to get benzene for the car, which was as important as to have the car. Because a car without fuel, I mean, it's nothing. And I became very close to the point that I start-- I had a lot of confidence in him and I was sure that he's not going to betray me. Because at my job that I was, at my-- at my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my rank and job, I had to be very, extremely careful because within-- within the system, within the Soviet system, I myself was investigated very often. And they tried to find out whether I have relatives abroad, and I always had to deny that, or to tell them that I don't have anybody abroad. And they-- they just-- that-- that's the way they are handling things. I mean, they're very careful. They're very investigative. And those who are investigating are all also being investigated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I became friendly. I used to go to them to visit them from time to time, whenever I had a chance. And in a conversation, I mentioned to him that I have relatives in Cuba. And I say, how can I get in touch with them? And he told me, look, why don't we go to the American sector? There is a joint. There is a HIAS. And maybe we can find out a little bit more about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took his word. We took the U-Bahn, which is the-- the metro. And we-- one day, we decided-- the day that I had-- was off, we decided to go and to-- which is not-- was not correct for me to do that, because without permission, I should not have crossed the borderline from one sector to the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: But knowing my head officer and then knowing the way they would react even if they catch me, I knew that they are not going to-- to react. I would just say that we took a tour or something, you know? And we went to the-- we came to the joint.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in, and here we met the American colonel, who was very nice, and he listened to me. And I was at a point to tell him that I would like to-- to join them to escape and to get out of Russia. I said, I'm a Russian officer. I would like to-- very much to go to Cuba rather than to be re-mobilized [INAUDIBLE]. He looked at me and first of all, a little bit suspicious. And he said to me, look, you know, we're in very good terms with the Russians and we don't want-- I don't want to create an international incident. I said, what kind of international-- I'm a little guy. I mean, it's-- you're just helping a person, and I am surprised that-- And I was the point to cry, you know, because I was-- I was so emotionally disturbed. That here's a Jewish person here in the American sector. How come he doesn't tell me, OK, take off your uniform. Take off your-- your-- although I went in civil. I went in-- and-- and you're free. We'll take you with the plane to-- to the-- to the American-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the American side, to the American zone of occupation. So I told Selton, let's go. I was-- I want to go. He said, just a minute. You said you had relatives in Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: He called his secretary. He said, what's their name? I said, Baikovits. You know their address? I said, no. I just know only they're in Havana. He says, I don't know if that's going to be enough. So he told me, you know what? I'm going to send a telegram, a cable, to your relatives that you're alive and that you are interested in being in touch with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave the order to his secretary. And I thought, this is just only way to make me feel better. We gave him Helmut Selton's address, because I wouldn't dare to give him any address for myself. Two weeks later, Helmut calls me. He said, [? Arkady, ?] I have some good news for you. Let's get together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, don't talk, because here everybody-- the walls have ears. I come over to him, and he says to me, I have a letter for you. I take the letter and I see it's written in Yiddish, from Cuba. I was amazed, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle was the head of the Jewish-- of the Jewish Federation in Cuba, of the Jewish community center there. I mean, he was the president and he received the letter. And he's writing me a beautiful letter saying that he would be-- first of all, he's happy to-- and that my other uncle, that was-- that I left when I escaped from the ghetto, found himself. He survived Dachau, and that my aunt survived the other concentration camp, and-- and their daughter is also with them. This was in 19-- in 1947. And they would be happy to do everything possible to help me out. And my uncle, the other-- the one that was liberated and he was already in Cuba at that time, wrote me a little note to say that my friend, Louis Braude, who was a-- who we were together in the brigades in the-- in the-- in the marmalade factory, and I helped him quite a lot because he was weak. I was much stronger than him-- that he is studying engineering in Munich and that he is going to write him right away, and he should help me to-- to-- to escape. To make the story short, two weeks later I received a letter from-- from Braude. Helmut calls me again. We have a-- and I remember I used to-- [INAUDIBLE] we used to hide the letters in the-- that was a-- a broken wallpaper part, and we used to drop them off there in the wallpaper, you know, in case they come to search him. And now they-- they should not, because he-- he could have been in as much trouble as myself, you know. And Braude call-- writes to me that he is very happy to find out that I'm alive and that he is ready-- he has already a person to send me a smuggler that's going to guide me out and help me to escape the-- the Russian army. But I should not tell him that I am a Russian officer. I should tell him that I am a smuggler or anything else. To make the story short, towards Passover, a guy comes, calls Selton. Selton calls me. We have a-- we-- he came with a whole family, with a wife and-- and-- and a child. And I met with him. Says-- I tell him I'm also a smuggler and I'm a good friend of this Braude, and that's the reason why he wants me to have him there, because I should help him. I tell him my-- all kind of story. He said, OK. Get yourself ready. Go to the Bahnhof and get yourself tickets. You, as an officer, you can get tickets. I-- I mean, me, who knows how-- how long we'll take. I took-- I put on the uniform. I had my motorcycle, had my most important belongings. I went to the-- I told them I need-- we were-- they were-- they were three. And then I say, I need four tickets to take the train deep in the Russian zone towards the British sec-- towards the British zone of occupation, towards Hamburg. Because he said that's the best way, and there is where the borders are not so carefully watched, you know between Britain and the-- the British and the Russian had a much better relationship than the-- than the Russians and the Americans. I followed his instructions. I got myself-- took away from my German officer that I took in-- in custody, I took away a little .22 caliber pistol. I had plenty of money in German mark. I had my Russian identification card and had my German identification card. And I said to myself, let's go. I bought-- I bought the tickets, dropped off my uniform, put everything in the motorcycle in the side wagon, put the motorcycle there somewhere in the side, because I didn't want to leave that in the apartment, [INAUDIBLE]. We took the train, and I said to myself-- it's a long-- a long trip with the train. I said to myself, there comes a-- a German patrol, I'm going to tell them Russian. If there comes a Russian patrol, I'm going to tell them I'm German. We come close to the border. All day long, the train was packed. You know, there were no-- no-- no room to breathe. Here comes in a patrol, a mixed patrol with German-- German police and the-- German police in there on the Russian border, and-- and Russians. I thought that that's the end, and I-- and I held my hand in my pocket, and I said to myself that they're not going to-- they're not going to get me alive because I know what-- what would happen to me if they would catch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- it was a spark of luck, you know? The child started-- when they came to ask me a question, the child starts crying because this guy was with his wife and child. He always traveled, you know. That's the way he smuggled things, you know? They covered it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was some attention. They asked me a question and I responded them in-- in-- in German, and where that I'm going to-- to visit some-- some people. And the child starts crying, and the Russian sol-- one of the head of the Russian came over and just quieted down the child. And this was this part. To make the story short, they kept checking the train. We came to the-- close to the border. We had to cross. He showed me how to zigzag and where to cross. We crossed the border and here we came in and we see a big sign that said, you're coming into the British zone of occupation. If you have some arms or have some weapons, you have to turn it into the British [? commendatore. ?] I take out my little gun. I-- I want to throw that away. First of all, I kiss the ground. I said, gee, that's the first time that I am truly liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, no. [LAUGHS] Give it to me. You know, that's a smuggler. He put it away. He says, especially so that the-- the elephant bone handles, so that is a very valuable pistol and all that. And then he asked me, how come you have a-- you carry weapons? I mean, that's not allowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I told him that I was-- I am a Russian officer. He say, if you would have told me that, I would never have taken you because I don't want to have to do anything with the-- with-- with soldiers. To make the story short, I-- we came to the-- we reached Hamburg. We slept over in the station there, and it was the-- the best-- the best night of sleep on-- in-- in the station, on the floor. From there, we went to Bergen-Belsen, the concentration camp which was there. And there, we had another man that guided me, that took me-- took over, and he took me to-- to Munich, to the American zone of occupation. My friend took me into his house. They sheltered me there. He had one-- the house, he had one little room, and he shared with me his food and everything. And then I went to-- to Feldafing. That was a displaced person camp. And there, I met this lady, the relative that I used to send her the packages. She was there. She was remarkably happy to-- to see me. And we start working. My uncles wrote me a letter now. I get in touch with them. They wrote a letter that they have a visa for me in France, in Paris, and that they I should-- I should try to get to Paris. I tried to reach France illegally with somebody that made illegal documents. They caught me, they took me off, and sent me back. And there is where I had certain belongings and I-- I threw them-- I threw them away because I was afraid they were going to find out that I'm a Russian officer and they might send me to the Ru-- After I came back, I started working very diligently. My aunt, the relative, helped me. And finally, I got a no man's passport from no man's land. And with this-- no, it was not a passport. It was a paper, an entry paper. And with this paper, I was able to reach France. And there in France, I spent a little time there, and joined the Jewish community and had ration there from the-- from-- in France. And I had a other friend-- a other gentleman that went to Cuba joined me. And he wanted to go-- he told me, wherever do you go, wherever you go, I am going to follow you. I took my visa, but I didn't have-- ah, yes, I was going to-- I went to the-- to the American consulate-- to the Cuban consulate. And he told me that we have to go via-- to-- in order to go to Cuba, we have to go via United States. And in order to go to-- to Cuba via United States, it would take six to eight months waiting period. I said, no. I don't have the patience. I start going. I-- I cannot wait so long. I'll find a way to reach Cuba without going to United States. I came to-- came back. I start going to the different agencies. I found finally a Air France maiden flight from a-- from a-- from Air France that was going to-- that a six-motor hydroplane that was going to-- to Dakar, and from Dakar to Antigua-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Dakar to Antigua.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I took this-- this flight was a fantastic flight. We went through Dakar, Africa. And then from there, we went to-- we went to British-- British Antigua. And in British Antigua, we found out that in order to go to-- to Cuba, I had to go through Puerto Rico, which is an American commonwealth, you know? And I need still a transit visa. But Antigua was a very small island with only one hotel, in the hotel that we stayed. And all we had together was $10, you know? We stayed. The lady, the owner, we told her our story and she went to see the consul. And the next day, he gave us the visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in. I show them that we-- I'm staying over in Puerto Rico only one night, and I have $10. I-- when I came out, I gave the $10 to my friend. He showed the same $10. And we came to Puerto Rico. We slept over there. And next thing, we tele-- sent a cable to my uncle in Cuba that, on August 1st, we're going to be in Cuba. And August 1st, he came to pick us up in Camaguey, not in Havana, because this flight, Pan American flight from Antigua to Camaguey, was-- was the flight that took us to Cuba. Ad I first met my-- my uncle, whom I didn't know, actually. Only by-- by references. The first thing, he took me to a department store and he brought me into the department store and said, take off all your clothes. And just I-- I got dressed, newly dressed. And then he told me, look in the mirror. And I didn't recognize myself. So that was my destiny in Cuba--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how you met your wife, because you looked so good now.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] That was destiny, Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you, in just--

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, in-- briefly, in Cuba I spent 13 years. I worked there up, and I was very successful in my business. And I actually acquired business. I had also some-- I was in the construction business. I made nice strides, you know, in the-- And then, apparently, the Russians came after me. And this is what I joke always. I said that it took them very short, took them only about 30 years to come over to Cuba and take over Cuba, and they had Castro. And I was already-- I got married in 1949. I met my beautiful wife with-- had two children. And we had to break up our life again and to come to-- to come to the United States. And in December-- I sent them out before that, ahead of time, because I tried to realize and to get out and send my little-- little money in order to be able to come to the States and not be a burden, not to-- to be a burden on the American government to subsidize me. So in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us just a little quick highlight of your success here in Miami Beach?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I start working for us. It was very difficult the first time. So I used to make $50 a week. First of all, I-- in the beginning, I used to read the ads in the paper to see whether-- I had also my father-in-law with my mother-in-law who didn't speak English, you know? I was the one that spoke a little better English. My wife spoke also a little bit. And it was very hard in the beginning. And we started. The community was very good. And then I start building. I found myself with some friends from South America, from Central America, from Guatemala, a partner and some friends from here. And they helped me to get on my feet. And I left the job that I had, which was-- I was not very happy with. And I started in the construction business. And year by year, I start improving. And then I felt that I have to give back something to the community, I felt, to get involved in-- in-- in politics. And truthfully, at that time when I ran for politics, there were not too many contenders because the situation here in Miami Beach was so bad that people didn't even want to run-- to run politics. And then I decided, and I was elected by a large majority by-- by speaking Spanish, and speaking Spanish helped me quite a lot. And I became the commissioner, which I had three more-- four successful re-election campaigns, which I was re-elected. I was vice mayor of the city. And I decided afterwards, in '93, to retire, so-- momentarily, because I wanted to go back my business. I have neglected, in a certain way, my business. I felt I gave eight years to the community, plus six years in the planning board. And I thought that was a good contribution of my part. And now we're here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been back to Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lithuania? I went back to Lithuania. I went-- I-- I found the grave of my grandmother. I don't like to go back there because I-- I found the same Lithuanians the way they were 50 years ago, and the same-- almost the same way. I also want to mention that I was appointed by the President Bush to the Holocaust, as a council member of the museum, for five years. And I am serving now in Washington as the-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] as a member of this prestigious Holocaust Museum in Washington, which is a governmental agency.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this photograph, please? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That one. That one.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the wedding-- the wedding of my parents in the '20s in our city of Rakiszki. That is, of course, my mother and father, my grandfather, grandmother, and uncles and aunts. Some of them, at a later date, came to Cuba, like my-- most of my uncles have immigrated in later-- in the late '20s, to Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo is in Palanga. This is a coastal city in Lithuania. And what you see there is the palace of a count, and my parents here. My father, Chaim Resnikowitz, Lisa Resnikowitz, my mother. My two sisters-- my older one, Yonina and Miriam. And my uncle, Solomon Baikovits, and myself on this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in the-- I would say in 1936. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken by myself. I was, like, 15 years old. I would say this was in 1938. That is my mother, Lisa, my sister, Yonina, and my sister, Miriam. And in background is the house in Rakiszki where we used to live in. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1937, and this is in Rakiszki. This is our house. This is the part of the veranda. And it happened to be that, 50 years later, I came back to Lithuania and I found the same house and the same veranda, almost the same-- in the same fashion, but only 50 years older. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in Kovno. Myself at a fire hydrant, and in the background, you see the gymnasia, [NON-ENGLISH]. The Real Gymnasium in Kovno, the gymnasium that I finished in 1941. And also, across the street was the upper house, which you can see only a little bit of. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It came off.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This-- this picture is very unique. I mean, this is 100 days before we finished the gymnasium, the Hebrew gymnasium in Kovno. And this is actually the ghetto with the student-- the students and the teachers-- the students and the teachers together 100 days before finishing our-- the-- finishing the gymnasium before entering the university. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a very unique picture. This is a group of Jews who were coming back from the city after work, with non-workers, coming back to the gate to the ghetto. And it happened to be that I found myself by-- by-- by recognizing the police officer that is right in front by the name of Aronstam who used to rough me up quite a few times. And looking at him, I found that I am part of this picture. And it was remarkable that I also found the photographer, George Kadish, who lives in Hollywood and who had the original. And he was able to tender me the picture and he inscribed, very graciously, about this incredible event. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is also-- it's also now at the museum, when you go through the section of the Kovno Ghetto and they have rotating pictures. One of them of is this-- precisely this picture. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is in the city of Anyksciai. There was a big stone by the name of Puntukas. And this is-- we had an outing with a group of friends from Rakiszki and we came especially and were on display. Have many good friends of mine who, unfortunately, only maybe one or two survived the-- the Holocaust. And this was in the '30s. All right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is in Berlin in 1947, before my escape from the Russian army. And it's remarkable that I was able to save this-- this picture. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is some rewards that I have-- memories I've gotten from the Russian army. We have here a medal for the liberation of Konigsberg, which became Kaliningrad afterwards. We have for liberation of Berlin. We have for the end of the war, and we have for-- for bravery. And in the bottom, you can see a cigarette box which was-- which I purchased in one of my trips to Nuremberg when I was at the Nuremberg process with Colonel Biriuk, who was my immediate [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] officer in the army. And what it says is Nuremberg-- Trial of Nuremberg, 19-- 194-- Trial of Nuremberg 1941-1945. But of course, the trial of Nuremberg was later. But mentioning those terrible years of the hegemony of the Nazi German-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Nazi German [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] extermination and liquidation program. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my immediate family. And truthfully, I have two daughter-in-laws who are considered-- I have never had a daughter, but my two daughter-in-laws are-- are as good, and maybe they're even better than my sons, which I shouldn't say that. That's my daughter-in-law, Fern, my son, Lionel-- Dr. Lionel Resnick-- my dear wife, Sarita, myself, my daughter-in-law-- daughter-- Lydia, and my son, Jimmy-- James Resnick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not showing my six grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken, I would say, in 1993. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yeah. This-- this here is my American grandchildren. This is Stephen, Andrew, David. My-- that's my son Jimmy's and Lydia's children. And then my son, Lionel, and Fern's children is Adam, Elizabeth, and Alexis. And, of course, my wife and myself.
